ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Masala Coke. FOODON_03301099 (carbonated beverage) is the closest defensible parent class. Masala Coke is a spiced variant of cola with added Indian spices.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN000A (Soft drinks, carbonated) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for spiced cola beverages in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Masala Coke or spiced carbonated cola beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Masala Coke is approximately 300 ml (one glass), as typically served at Indian restaurants and street food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated soft drink, cola type — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14400) adapted with spice additions; spice contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for chaat masala and black salt
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Masala Coke is a cola-based beverage with added Indian spices (chaat masala, black salt, cumin, lemon juice). Base cola nutrient values are taken from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174851, Carbonated beverage, cola, without caffeine, as a representative cola). Spice additions at typical recipe proportions (approximately 1–2 g chaat masala per 300 ml serving) contribute negligible macronutrients but add sodium from black salt. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect black salt and chaat masala contribution. Per-100g values are calculated from the blended recipe estimate. ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 used for spice micronutrient reference.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, regular cola — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson FS et al., 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Masala Coke. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for regular cola soft drink (Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, et al., International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Masala Coke is a cola base with spice additions; the spices (chaat masala, black salt, cumin) do not materially alter the glycemic response of the beverage. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 28.5) / 100 = 15.1, where carbohydrate per 300 ml serving = 9.5 g/100g × 3 = 28.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from black salt and chaat masala; added sugar from cola base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Masala Coke consumption due to the combined sodium load from black salt (kala namak) and chaat masala, which can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake, particularly if consumed in large or repeated servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content from cola base; moderate glycemic index of 53
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Masala Coke with caution, as the cola base delivers approximately 28.5 g of free sugars per standard 300 ml serving, which can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise despite the moderate GI of 53.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and cholesterol; moderate free sugar and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Masala Coke contains no fat or cholesterol and poses a low direct cardiovascular risk from lipids, but regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk; occasional intake is preferable for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Coke is a popular Indian street-food beverage made by combining cola (typically Coca-Cola or Pepsi) with a blend of Indian spices, most commonly chaat masala, black salt (kala namak), roasted cumin powder (jeera), and a squeeze of fresh lemon juice. Optional additions include ginger juice, mint leaves, and ice. The drink is served chilled and is widely enjoyed across India and at Indian restaurants in Singapore and other diaspora communities. The flavour profile is a distinctive sweet-sour-salty-spicy combination: the sweetness and effervescence of the cola is cut by the tartness of lemon, the sulphurous mineral depth of black salt, and the warm earthiness of cumin and chaat masala. The carbonation remains prominent, giving the drink a lively, refreshing character. Nutritionally, Masala Coke is primarily a sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage with approximately 37 kcal per 100 ml, derived almost entirely from the free sugars in the cola base (approximately 9.2 g sugar per 100 ml). Protein and fat are effectively absent. The spice additions contribute trace micronutrients and a modest sodium increment from black salt. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-moderate range) based on the cola base, with a glycemic load of approximately 15 per standard 300 ml serving. Clinically, the primary concerns are free sugar intake relevant to glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes, and sodium from spice additions relevant to hypertension. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is generally halal-compliant.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Masala Coke?
Is Masala Coke good for diabetics?
What spices are in Masala Coke?
What is the sugar content of Masala Coke?
Can I drink Masala Coke if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Masala Coke exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cola data (FDC 174851) with spice contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 reference data. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database cola entry. Confidence is medium because the base cola data is well-supported but the spice-adjusted composite is an estimation.
notes: Masala Coke is a composite beverage; no single approved source provides a direct nutritional entry. Per-100g values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central carbonated cola data with spice additions estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. protein_g is naturally zero as this is a sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero by formulation — cola and the spice additions at typical recipe quantities contain no fat. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero or effectively zero for this beverage type. Sodium is elevated above plain cola baseline to reflect black salt and chaat masala contribution (estimated +15 mg/100g above plain cola). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported for this beverage type in the source databases and their contribution from spice additions at recipe quantities is below meaningful detection thresholds. GI set to 53 (low category, ≤55) based on analogous cola entry from University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). GL per serving = (53 × 28.5) / 100 = 15.1 for a 300 ml serving. Serving at Dugout - Circular Road (field survey data) priced at SGD 5.00.

Applied mandatory multilingual naming for Indian cuisine in Singapore context: Chinese (simplified + traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
